does: This paper develops the Layered Attack Surface Model (LASM), a structural taxonomy for organizing security threats in agentic AI systems. Rather than categorizing attacks by type, LASM decomposes the agentic stack into seven layers spanning foundation models, cognition, memory, tool execution, multi-agent coordination, ecosystem integration, and governance. The authors augment this spatial decomposition with a temporal axis distinguishing instantaneous, session-persistent, cross-session cumulative, and sub-session-stack threats. By systematically analyzing 116 papers from 2021–2026 through this 7×4 framework, the paper maps the landscape of security vulnerabilities unique to agentic systems, revealing where threats originate and over what timescales they manifest.
connects: This work extends prior security research on LLMs beyond stateless prompt-based systems to address the qualitatively different threat surface introduced by agency. It builds on existing prompt injection and jailbreaking taxonomies while acknowledging their limitations for capturing architectural vulnerabilities. The paper synthesizes emerging literature on agentic systems that persist memory across sessions, delegate authority to tools, coordinate among multiple agents, and operate over extended horizons. It responds to the gap between foundational LLM security work and the novel attack vectors enabled by persistence, tool use, and multi-agent delegation, integrating insights from system security, supply-chain risk, and distributed systems into the agentic AI domain.
enables: This framework enables future work by providing a shared vocabulary and structure for analyzing agentic security. It allows researchers and practitioners to pinpoint where in the stack defenses are needed and what threat timescales matter most, facilitating targeted countermeasures rather than generic hardening. The taxonomy clarifies research gaps and priorities, supporting development of layer-specific defenses for memory poisoning, tool hijacking, multi-agent exploitation, and governance failure. By mapping the threat landscape systematically, the paper sets up systematic security evaluation methodologies for agentic systems and guides the design of agents that account for their expanded attack surface from the ground up.
